data.title: Black Hills: How Defensive Is Your Utility?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

To mitigate tariffs and geopolitical risks, I focus on stocks nearing inflection points, with tailwinds strong enough to cut through the macroeconomic noise.

On the other hand, many investors are rushing toward utilities to shelter from expected volatility. Surging AI data center demand adds a growth dimension, renewing interest in the sector. One example is Black Hills Corporation (NYSE:BKH).

This piece concludes that BKH

  1. isn't the optimal investment to capitalize on the AI data center trends
  2. isn't the most effective hedge against market volatility

BKH Has Historically Underperformed During Recessions and Economic Disruptions

During recessions and economic turbulence, BKH typically underperforms its peers - represented by the S&P 500 Utilities Sector Index (XLU). More importantly, its value as a market hedge is almost non-existent when compared to the S&P 500 index (SPX) and the Russell 3000 (RUA).

Period: 2001 dot-com bubble

BKH underperformed XLU, RUA, and SPX

Period: 2008 Great Recession

BKH underperformed XLU and performed in line with SPX and RUA

Period: 2015 shale-boom-bust

BKH underperformed XLU, SPX, and RUA

Period: Q1 2020 Coronavirus Pandemic

BKH underperformed XLU and marginally outperformed SPX and RUA

Profitability

Nearly 94% of BKH's assets are regulated, creating predictable returns. State Energy Commissions set the electric and gas rates that BKH can charge households. The rates are reviewed annually, ensuring that BKH investors receive a fair return on assets "ROA", running between 2.7% and 2.9% since 2020, as shown in the chart below.

BKH needs to ask permission from regulators before expanding its asset base. The capital structure (equity vs debt) is also regulated by the state. Through authorized leverage, BKH now realizes between 8% and 10% Return on Equity "ROE".

Given Inflation, is BKH's 4.7% Yield Worth It?

Some investors are questioning the value of BKH's 4.7% dividend yield given inflation rates, which currently stand at roughly 3%, and the returns on the 10-year treasury, which stand around 4.4%.

The dividend spread - BKH's dividend yield minus 10-year Treasury yield - is only 0.3%, a narrow equity risk premium. The inflation-adjusted real yield is 2.7% and has historically fallen below zero in periods of high inflation, as shown in the chart below.

However, this approach to measuring the value of BKH's return is flawed because it doesn't account for the earnings that BKH generates but keeps funding its growth.

A more suitable measure of real returns to shareholders is the earnings yield - (EPS/Share Price) which currently stands at roughly 7.9%, demonstrating a reasonable compensation for shareholders, even when accounting for inflation and risk-free rates. This is expected because State and Federal Energy Commissions incorporate inflation and risk-free rates when establishing the authorized ROE rates for BKH.

The Data Center Growth Myth

Some analysts hype BKH's growth prospects, pointing to rising demand from AI data centers. Although data centers are expanding and consuming more energy, framing this as a major growth driver is misleading.

First, none of the states that BKH operates is particularly a data center hub. Wyoming, South Dakota, and Montana aren't on the digital economy map, while Colorado is a secondary data center market at best.

Second, BKH's strategic positioning doesn't align with the clean energy mandates of many large corporations. The company generates more than a third of its electricity from coal and owns a coal mine. Data center operators in Colorado appear more enthusiastic about partnering with Xcel Energy than BKH. Xcel's target long-term EPS growth is between 6% - 8%, double the growth target of BKH, despite having a slightly above payout ratio.

Third, the contribution of data centers to BKH revenue is too small at this point, and its growth will barely move the needle and will stay as such for at least the next 5 years. BKH expects data center contribution to earnings to double between now and 2028, rising exponentially to potentially reach 10% of revenue in 2030. This suggests a modest current contribution.

Sustainable Growth Rate

The sustainable level of growth is essentially the level of the authorized ROE multiplied by the retained earnings. The company targets a payout ratio of 55% to 65%, retaining 35% to 45%. Based on an authorized ROE between 9% and 10%, the company's sustainable EPS growth falls between 3.15% and 4.5%. That's exactly what the company told investors to expect.

Dividend Stability

Generally, if BKH's expenses rise, it can pass the costs to the consumer. As long as management remains competent and compliant with risk mitigation guidelines, BKH will recover whatever cost it incurs, plus an agreed-upon return, enough to keep investors (and consumers) happy. This balance is critical to ensure continuous, reliable, and safe electric and gas service.

The main risk is the timing of unexpected costs and utility rate hikes to recover these costs. This timing gap creates profitability fluctuations, which, in rare cases, have been significant. Currently, BKH has multiple applications pending to raise prices to recover past costs it incurred last year. However, Moody's analysts note that BKH operates in states that are responsive to utility companies' needs. Despite some contention, and even protests from some town councilors in the past, I think the company has a good relationship with Energy Commissions, as evident in the rate increases that led to these town council-level protests in the first place.

A case in point is BKH's coal assets, which represent a third of its electricity generation capacity. In most sectors, this exposure would be worrying. But here? Not so much. Every plant is authorized by regulators and almost guaranteed a fair ROE via rate reviews. If regulators wish to transition to more clean energy, BKH has the talent to embark on such initiatives, fully paid for by households and commercial consumers.

Final Thoughts and Risks

BKH perhaps trades at the lower bound of the industry range, with a Valuation Quant Score of B+, but I wouldn't particularly call it undervalued. Compared to many of its peers, the lack of a clear sustainable energy roadmap excludes it, or at least puts it back in the line of many data center operators' procurement processes.

In terms of growth, the company has a Growth Quant Score of D, putting it in the 86th percentile among 53 of its peers in the gas, electric, and multi-utility industries. For this reason, I don't believe that BKH is undervalued, nor do I believe that we should expect an imminent expansion in its valuation multiples. The main risk to this hypothesis is a structural and sustainable shift in the environmental policies and attitudes in the United States, particularly towards legacy operators with stable generation capacity, such as BKH.

data.title: Whispers Of Cuts, Roars Of Chips
data.description:
Market Report for Thursday, June 26, 2025

Markets rallied Thursday, brushing off Washington’s procedural theater and the fading fiction that anyone still tracks the Byrd Bath, in favor of what really matters: AI, chips, and the transformation that’s no longer coming - it’s here.

Level Change 6/26/25 (%)

+0.9 Dow
+1.0 Nasdaq
+0.9 Nasdaq 100
+0.8 S&P 500
+1.3 S&P 400
+1.6 S&P 600

Washington kept up appearances with high drama over the reconciliation bill, still marinating in Senate procedure. Medicaid cuts, SALT caps, and the legislative equivalent of ghost pepper seasoning had everyone sweating. Don’t fall for it: passage is all but baked in. They’ll get their overspending done.

In a parallel theater, the administration hinted at an imminent trade twist, in which the EU relaxes both tariffs and non-tariff barriers. Treasury, in turn, scrapped its oddly personal “revenge tax” in exchange for G7 allies shelving their own retaliatory levies. The press secretary, in full Zen mode, waved off the July 9 deadline as more suggestion than schedule.

But the real signal came from the Fed, or rather, from the White House’s interpretation of it. President Trump is still considering naming a replacement for Chair Jerome Powell months before his term ends next May. The Fed chair, having stubbornly clung to his independence and economic models, now faces political replacement therapy.

Fedsters played it cool in monetary Esperanto.

Goolsbee said early naming wouldn’t rattle the committee. Daly and Collins advised patience. Barkin warned that trade policy resolution could drag on. Barr said the Fed is positioned to wait. The question is: wait for what? It used to be data. Now it looks more like they’re waiting for the Oval hammer to fall on Powell’s head. Adjustable-rate mortgage morons want him gone, data be damned.

But none of that really matters. Not the timing of cuts, not who chaired what. The only story with shelf life is AI.

Which brings us to Micron (MU -1.3%), which beat and raised on strong demand for DRAM and HBM chips. DRAM is the workhorse memory that brings multitasking to life. HBM - high-bandwidth memory - is the newer, faster, fancier cousin, built for AI models that like their data served hot and in bulk.

Micron’s earnings call was basically a mixtape for the AI crowd: accelerating demand, limited supply, pricing power. The market responded by bidding up the entire chip complex, with Nvidia (NVDA) touching another record again. No one remembers recession calls when AI chips are printing cash and guidance is going vertical.

And that was the day: Powell threatened, Powell ignored, tariffs teased to ease, and AI marching oblivious to it all. The macro crowd might notice when it’s Fed Chair GPT.

Original Post
